documents.title: Headstage (Part 1)
documents.text:
Headstage
The KonteX X-Headstage™ series is a family of high-performance neural recording interfaces designed to provide stable, low-noise signal acquisition across a wide range of experimental configurations.
Engineered for both head-fixed and freely moving animal studies, X-Headstages combine compact mechanical design, flexible connectivity, and scalable channel configurations to support modern electrophysiology research.

documents.title: Headstage (Part 2)
documents.text:
Engineered for both head-fixed and freely moving animal studies, X-Headstages combine compact mechanical design, flexible connectivity, and scalable channel configurations to support modern electrophysiology research.
The series is fully compatible with the XDAQ acquisition platform and supports integration with multiple probe technologies and adapter ecosystems.

documents.title: XeR16-HDMI-ACC (Part 1)
documents.text:
XeR16-HDMI-ACC
2026-06-26
Overview
E-series X-Headstage™ is designed to offer the best value while maintaining the flexibility of the X-Headstage™ that can be used with several off-the-shelf probe adapters. The advantage of adapter based approach can increase the utilization and longevity of headstage usage. If the probe facing connector breaks due to daily wear, it can be easily and cheaply replaced.

Motion Tracking

documents.title: XeR16-HDMI-ACC (Part 5)
documents.text:
Motion Tracking
All XR Headstages are equipped with a built-in analog 3-axis accelerometer as a standard feature. The accelerometer is sampled at the same rate as the headstage input channels, and its data are available through the AUX1, AUX2, and AUX3 channels in the recorded data stream. The analog 3-axis accelerometer is included with all XR Headstages and cannot be omitted.

documents.title: XeR16-HDMI-ACC (Part 6)
documents.text:
All XR Headstages are equipped with a built-in analog 3-axis accelerometer as a standard feature. The accelerometer is sampled at the same rate as the headstage input channels, and its data are available through the AUX1, AUX2, and AUX3 channels in the recorded data stream. The analog 3-axis accelerometer is included with all XR Headstages and cannot be omitted.
For applications requiring active cable rotation compensation, an optional IMU Sensor is available. When used in conjunction with a KonteX Commutator, the IMU Sensor provides real-time rotational motion data that allows the Commutator to automatically rotate according to the animal's movement, minimizing cable twisting during freely moving experiments. The IMU Sensor is intended for Commutator-based motion compensation and is not part of the standard headstage configuration.

documents.title: XeR16-HDMI-ACC (Part 7)

XSR Headstages does not support 3-axis accelerometer.

documents.title: X3SR32-HDMI (Part 1)
documents.text:
X3SR32-HDMI
2026-04-13
Overview
The X3SR32 is a 32-channel stim-record X-Headstage™ developed by KONTEX, designed for simultaneous neural stimulation and recording in electrophysiological research. Based on two RHS2116 chips from Intan Technologies, the X3SR32 delivers reliable bidirectional data acquisition and output performance in a compact and robust form factor.
X3RS32_HDMI Overview
Dimension and weight
Weight ~1.1g
unit: mm
Armor

documents.title: X3SR32-HDMI (Part 2)
documents.text:
Armor
Optional protective covers are available for each headstage, offering extra protection and anchoring. These covers, crafted from Polyoxymethylene (POM), are lightweight and resistant to chemicals. There are three standard armor types, with options for customization in shape and material to suit diverse experimental requirements. It's important to note that the size, weight, and shape of these armors will differ across various X-Headstage™ models.
S Armor

documents.title: X3SR32-HDMI (Part 3)
documents.text:
S Armor
The S armors with ear loops around the side used to anchor headstage to the accessories such as electrode adapters.
M Armor
The M armors are designed to provide protection while maintaining minimal size.
IP Armor
The IP armors are designed to provide the most protection and fully encase the X-Headstage. The X-Headstage™ with the optional parylene coating has been tested to the IP68 standard.
Compatible Acquisition System

documents.title: X3SR32-HDMI (Part 4)
documents.text:
Compatible Acquisition System
The X3SR32 X-Headstage™ is designed for seamless integration with the KONTEX XDAQ™ system, ensuring synchronized stimulation and recording with high fidelity. In addition to the XDAQ™, the X3SR32 is also compatible with a range of open-source and proprietary acquisition systems, provided appropriate signal remapping or interfacing is used. Below is a list of confirmed compatible systems:
Intan Recording Controller (512ch or 1024ch)
Intan Stim-Record Controller

documents.title: X3SR32-HDMI (Part 5)
documents.text:
Intan Stim-Record Controller
Open-Ephys acquisition box
NeuroNexus Smartbox
Key Features
32 Channels
Enables concurrent recording and stimulation on up to 32 channels.
Dual RHS2116 Architecture
Implements two RHS2116 integrated circuits, each supporting 16 channels, providing flexible and high-fidelity analog front-end performance.
Compact Design
Engineered for minimal weight and size, optimizing for head-mounted or implant-based setups.
Connector

documents.title: X3SR32-HDMI (Part 6)
documents.text:
Connector
Equipped exclusively with a micro-HDMI connector to interface with the XDAQ™ system.
Protective Cover Option
Can be optionally configured with a protective cover for enhanced durability and anchoring support during in vivo experiments.

The X6R line is KonteX’s high-channel-count X-Headstage™, engineered for advanced neural recording applications requiring 128 channels or more. It's important to note that the size, weight, and shape of these armors will differ across various X-Headstage™ models.

documents.title: SyncLink (Part 1)
documents.text:
SyncLink
2026-04-17
Overview
The primary purpose of the SyncLink is to enable precise time-step synchronization between multiple XDAQ systems. This is especially critical when performing simultaneous XR and XSR experiments on the same subject, which require the use of separate XDAQ units.
By synchronizing multiple XDAQs, the SyncLink ensures that data acquired from different headstage types are time-aligned, enabling accurate integration and analysis across systems.
Components

documents.title: SyncLink (Part 2)
documents.text:
Components
Dimension and Weight
Weight 230g
unit: mm
Device Overview
① Sync Ports (IN / OUT) :
Each SyncLink is equipped with Sync IN and Sync OUT ports.
SyncLinks are interconnected using standard Ethernet cables.
The IN / OUT configuration allows synchronization signals to be distributed across multiple SyncLinks in a daisy-chain.
② Harp Port :
A 3.5 mm audio jack is provided for connection to the Harp Clock Synchronizer.

documents.title: SyncLink (Part 3)
documents.text:
A 3.5 mm audio jack is provided for connection to the Harp Clock Synchronizer.
An adjacent status LED indicates the operational state of the Harp Port.
③ Expansion Port :
The Expansion Port is used to connect a KonteX IO Expander, allowing extended I/O functionality as required by the experimental setup.
④ XDAQ Expansion Ports :
EXP1/2 (HDMI): Connects the SyncLink to an XDAQ system.
These ports enable synchronization signal exchange between the SyncLink and connected XDAQ units.

documents.title: SyncLink (Part 4)
documents.text:
These ports enable synchronization signal exchange between the SyncLink and connected XDAQ units.
⑤ Ground Ports :
SYS GND (4 mm) — The digital ground shared by the internal electronic circuitry.
Chassis GND (4 mm) — Connected to the metal enclosure, the outer shield of the HDMI cable, and earth ground via the power supply.

documents.title: SyncLink (Part 5)
documents.text:
Chassis GND (4 mm) — Connected to the metal enclosure, the outer shield of the HDMI cable, and earth ground via the power supply.
As a starting point, we recommend bonding SYS GND and Chassis GND together and using this common node to ground the Faraday cage shielding. Since environmental conditions can vary significantly, users should evaluate and adjust the grounding configuration to determine the most effective setup for their specific environment.
LED Indicators

documents.title: SyncLink (Part 6)
documents.text:
LED Indicators
The SyncLink is equipped with multiple LEDs to indicate power and synchronization status:
① Power LED :
Indicates that the SyncLink is powered on.
② S1 LED :
Indicates the status of Synchronization Channel 1.
③ S2 LED :
Indicates the status of Synchronization Channel 2.
④ Harp Port LED :
Indicates the connection and activity status of the Harp port.
Getting Started
1. XDAQ and PC Setup

documents.title: SyncLink (Part 7)
documents.text:
1. XDAQ and PC Setup
Connect one SyncLink to one XDAQ, and connect each XDAQ to its corresponding PC. Each SyncLink operates as the synchronization interface for its associated XDAQ–PC pair.
2. Interconnecting Sync SyncLinks
To synchronize multiple XDAQ systems, connect the SyncLinks using standard Ethernet cables via the Sync Ports.
3. Sync Port Configuration
On the SyncLink connected to the first XDAQ, connect the Ethernet cable to the Sync Port (OUT).

documents.title: SyncLink (Part 8)
documents.text:
On the SyncLink connected to the first XDAQ, connect the Ethernet cable to the Sync Port (OUT).
Connect the other end of the Ethernet cable to the Sync Port (IN) on the SyncLink connected to the second XDAQ.
4. Expanding the Sync Chain
Additional SyncLinks can be connected in the same OUT-to-IN configuration to synchronize more XDAQ systems as required.
5. System Verification

documents.title: SyncLink (Part 9)
documents.text:
5. System Verification
Power on all SyncLinks, XDAQ systems, and PCs. Verify proper synchronization using the status LEDs on each Sync Box before starting data acquisition.

documents.title: SAdpt-SR64 (Part 1)
documents.text:
SAdpt-SR64
2026-04-11
Overview
The SR64 Adapter is essential for the proper operation of the X3SR64 Headstage. It features active electronic circuitry that conditions and merges headstage communication, which typically requires two separate HDMI cables. Unlike the standard X3SR32 headstage, which uses LVDS signal lines that require double the number of signal pins, the SR64 Adapter converts LVDS signal pairs into single-ended CMOS signals.

documents.title: SAdpt-SR64 (Part 2)
documents.text:
The SR64 Adapter is essential for the proper operation of the X3SR64 Headstage. It features active electronic circuitry that conditions and merges headstage communication, which typically requires two separate HDMI cables. Unlike the standard X3SR32 headstage, which uses LVDS signal lines that require double the number of signal pins, the SR64 Adapter converts LVDS signal pairs into single-ended CMOS signals.
This allows it to drive four RHS2116 chips, which make up the X3SR64 headstage, using a single KonteX HDMI cable. The adapter has been meticulously designed and tested to ensure the reliable performance of the X3SR64 Headstage.

The Hardware Short Circuit Analyzer is a high-performance device engineered for efficient and precise short circuit testing, supporting up to 256-channel combinations. Overview

documents.title: Inst-BrainSim (Part 2)
documents.text:
Overview
The BrainWave Simulator is a precision signal-generation platform designed to emulate neural activity for end-to-end validation and calibration of neural acquisition systems—from electrodes and headstages to acquisition platforms (e.g., XDAQ and others) and integrated experimental I/O setups. Its compact form factor, battery-powered operation, and dual-channel programmable outputs provide researchers with a reliable solution for bench-top testing and in-lab system verification—without the need for live tissue samples or biological inputs.

documents.title: Inst-BrainSim (Part 3)
documents.text:
The BrainWave Simulator is a precision signal-generation platform designed to emulate neural activity for end-to-end validation and calibration of neural acquisition systems—from electrodes and headstages to acquisition platforms (e.g., XDAQ and others) and integrated experimental I/O setups. Its compact form factor, battery-powered operation, and dual-channel programmable outputs provide researchers with a reliable solution for bench-top testing and in-lab system verification—without the need for live tissue samples or biological inputs.
As part of our continuous product enhancement strategy, the BrainWave Simulator has been refined to incorporate expanded signal source options, improved electrical integration, and streamlined control features. These improvements are evolutionary rather than disruptive, ensuring that all devices retain full compatibility with existing workflows while extending their applicability to more advanced experimental paradigms.

documents.text:
Commutator
KonteX offers both passive and motorized commutator designs to support diverse electrophysiological paradigms.Engineered for minimal breakaway torque and seamless multi-modal integration, our commutators deliver high performance and usability while providing exceptional value for advanced neuroscience research.

documents.title: Commutator (Part 2)
documents.text:
KonteX offers both passive and motorized commutator designs to support diverse electrophysiological paradigms.Engineered for minimal breakaway torque and seamless multi-modal integration, our commutators deliver high performance and usability while providing exceptional value for advanced neuroscience research.
Our newly redesigned non-motorized commutator delivers enhanced signal quality and low friction operation that are improved over the previous generation. It features integrated animal-side cable management for secure mounting and convenient cable storage. This versatile design supports both XR (recording only, up to 256 channels per cable) and XSR (stimulation-recording, up to 32 channels per cable) headstages, providing a streamlined solution for diverse experimental needs.

documents.title: Commutator (Part 3)

Our newly redesigned non-motorized commutator delivers enhanced signal quality and low friction operation that are improved over the previous generation. It features integrated animal-side cable management for secure mounting and convenient cable storage. This versatile design supports both XR (recording only, up to 256 channels per cable) and XSR (stimulation-recording, up to 32 channels per cable) headstages, providing a streamlined solution for diverse experimental needs.
The Commutator PRO is our most advanced and highly customizable model, featuring a built-in low-noise motor and two flexible torque sensing options: an animal-side gyroscope or an integrated Hall sensor. Its onboard microprocessor continuously and instantaneously detects and compensates for cable torque generated by animal movement, ensuring stable recordings and naturalistic behavior.

documents.title: Commutator PRO (Part 1)
documents.text:
Commutator PRO
2026-06-16
Overview

documents.title: Commutator PRO (Part 2)
documents.text:
Overview
The Commutator Pro is a high-performance, motorized commutator designed to prevent tether tangling during neuroscience experiments, ensuring smooth and uninterrupted behavioral recordings. It can operate using external power or its integrated, rechargeable lithium battery, offering flexible deployment options. A built-in microprocessor, working in tandem with a rotary magnetic sensor and/ or an external gyroscopic sensor, actively manages cable orientation by automatically counteracting twists.

documents.title: Commutator PRO (Part 3)
documents.text:
The Commutator Pro is a high-performance, motorized commutator designed to prevent tether tangling during neuroscience experiments, ensuring smooth and uninterrupted behavioral recordings. It can operate using external power or its integrated, rechargeable lithium battery, offering flexible deployment options. A built-in microprocessor, working in tandem with a rotary magnetic sensor and/ or an external gyroscopic sensor, actively manages cable orientation by automatically counteracting twists.
The system is highly configurable, supporting up to four Neuropixels ports alongside a conventional probe driven by either recording or stim-record headstages, and offers up to nine GPIO channels for user-defined applications. Its adaptable mounting and cable management system, complemented by an optional passthrough channel, enables seamless integration across a wide range of experimental setups—providing robust performance and maximum versatility.

documents.title: Commutator PRO (Part 4)
documents.text:
The system is highly configurable, supporting up to four Neuropixels ports alongside a conventional probe driven by either recording or stim-record headstages, and offers up to nine GPIO channels for user-defined applications. Its adaptable mounting and cable management system, complemented by an optional passthrough channel, enables seamless integration across a wide range of experimental setups—providing robust performance and maximum versatility.
Key Features
① Dual sensor options

documents.title: Commutator PRO (Part 5)
documents.text:
① Dual sensor options
Gyroscopic sensor
Ideal for seamless behavioral integration, especially in small animal setups. Can be easily adapted.
Magnetic sensor
Integrated magnetic encoder detects rotation without external sensors. Recommended for rats and non-Neuropixels experiments.
② Neuropixels & passive probe support
Supports up to 4 Neuropixels headstage ports. Each headstage supports either 1 Neuropixels 1.0 probe or 2 Neuropixels 2.0 probes.

documents.title: Commutator PRO (Part 6)
documents.text:
Supports up to 4 Neuropixels headstage ports. Each headstage supports either 1 Neuropixels 1.0 probe or 2 Neuropixels 2.0 probes.
Supports simultaneous Neuropixels and passive probe (e.g. electrical stimulation) operation.
③ Integrated sensor cable
Lightweight (< 1g) and highly flexible 34 AWG wire bundle.
Compatible with all existing headstage setup.
④ Optional fluidic/fiber passthrough channel

documents.title: Commutator PRO (Part 7)
documents.text:
④ Optional fluidic/fiber passthrough channel
5 mm inner diameter channel enables optical fiber or fluidic tubing access during ongoing experiments.
⑤ Low Operation acoustic noise
33-35dB. Measured at 30cm away using iPhone 16 Pro (Note: ambient is measured to be 32dB).
⑥ GPIO & PC control
Up to 9 GPIO lines for custom application.
Up to 9 GPIO lines for custom application.
Left/right override via PC or hardware panel.
⑦ Intelligent Battery Management

documents.title: Commutator PRO (Part 8)
documents.text:
⑦ Intelligent Battery Management
System power is supplied through the battery at all times. An onboard controller maintains the battery between 20% and 80% charge to help extend battery lifespan.
For applications requiring maximum runtime, intelligent battery management can be disabled through the GUI UART Control App, allowing the battery to remain fully charged at 100% capacity.

documents.title: Commutator PRO (Part 10)
documents.text:
LED Indicators
The KonteX Commutator Pro features several LED indicators designed to provide real-time visual feedback on device power status, connectivity, control mode, and battery level. Understanding these indicators is essential for proper setup and reliable operation in laboratory environments.
② Power indicator LED (White ring light)
Function:
Indicates that adequate power is supplied to the commutator (the motor drive system) when the main ON-OFF toggle button is toggled to ON.

documents.title: Commutator PRO (Part 11)
documents.text:
Indicates that adequate power is supplied to the commutator (the motor drive system) when the main ON-OFF toggle button is toggled to ON.
Behavior:
Solid white:
The device is powered and functioning normally.
Off:
The commutator is powered off or not receiving sufficient input voltage.
③ PC connection status LED
Function:
Indicates the status of USB-C communication with a host computer.
Behavior:
Solid light ON:

documents.title: Commutator PRO (Part 12)
documents.text:
Solid light ON:
The commutator is connected to a PC and has entered Remote Control Mode. In this mode, Sensitivity and Speed are governed by software settings displayed on the PC interface. Manual knob control on the front panel is overridden.
Off:
No active USB connection or operating in local manual mode (hardware knob control applies).

documents.text:

When it reaches the end of its lifespan—evidenced by rapid charging and immediate shutdown upon disconnecting external power—the device remains fully functional when powered externally. To replace the battery, please return the unit for servicing.

Getting Started

documents.title: Commutator PRO (Part 16)
documents.text:
Getting Started
Step 1: Powering the commutator
The Commutator Pro can be powered via either the USB-C port or the DC jack. When using the USB-C port—particularly during remote operation under PC control—ensure a stable 5V, 1A power supply, compliant with USB 3.0 or USB Power Delivery (USB-PD) specifications. Powering the device with only 5V, 0.5A (typical of USB 2.0) may result in unreliable performance, including failure to power on or disruptions in motion tracking.
Battery charging

documents.title: Commutator PRO (Part 17)
documents.text:
Battery charging
If your Commutator Pro is equipped with an internal battery (models designated with -B), the battery will begin charging automatically when power is connected. During charging, the front-panel battery level indicator LEDs will flash and illuminate progressively to reflect the current charge level, indicating active charging (see LED Indicators section for details).

documents.title: Commutator PRO (Part 18)
documents.text:
If your Commutator Pro is equipped with an internal battery (models designated with -B), the battery will begin charging automatically when power is connected. During charging, the front-panel battery level indicator LEDs will flash and illuminate progressively to reflect the current charge level, indicating active charging (see LED Indicators section for details).
Once fully charged, all four power LEDs will remain steadily illuminated. The Commutator Pro is equipped with an integrated Battery Management System (BMS) that prevents overcharging, making it safe to leave the device plugged in during operation.

documents.title: Commutator PRO (Part 19)
documents.text:
Once fully charged, all four power LEDs will remain steadily illuminated. The Commutator Pro is equipped with an integrated Battery Management System (BMS) that prevents overcharging, making it safe to leave the device plugged in during operation.
A full charge takes approximately 7 hours, and under normal operating conditions, the device can run for up to 6-9 hours on a full charge.

To connect the headstage cable to the Commutator Pro, locate the headstage port(s) positioned on the underside of the device, which serves as the rotating interface of the commutator.

When connecting the headstage cable to the Commutator Pro, the signal pathway begins at the rotating interface and exits through the fixed-side rotor side ports. After selecting the appropriate rotor side port (e.g., HDMI or Neuropixels), the user may route the cable toward the downstream recording amplifier.
To ensure stable cable handling during rotation and prevent signal artifacts caused by tension or displacement, proper cable management is essential.

To ensure stable cable handling during rotation and prevent signal artifacts caused by tension or displacement, proper cable management is essential.
Route the cable as illustrated below and securely lock the cable using the integrated screw clips, ensuring that adequate slack is maintained to prevent tension on the headstage connection and to allow smooth rotation of the commutator.

The Commutator Pro can be driven using a compatible animal side IMU sensor or the integrated magnetic encoder. Note: the IMU sensor found in the X-Headstage or Intan stock recording headstages is not compatible with the Commutator Pro.

Magnetic Sensor Sensitivity & Speed Controls
When using the KonteX Commutator Pro with magnetic sensor, two physical adjustment knobs labeled Sensitivity and Speed are available on the control panel. These knobs allow users to manually tune the system’s behavior based on the movement profile of the experimental subject.
① Sensitivity knob
Function:

documents.title: Commutator PRO (Part 37)
documents.text:
Function:
Adjusts the magnetic sensor sensitivity (7 increments) of the Hall effect sensor.
Effect:
Higher sensitivity increases the system’s responsiveness to small rotational movements of the tether, triggering sensor compensation with less motion input.
Usage scenario:
Small animals (e.g., mice) or low-activity experiments:
Turn the sensitivity knob clockwise to increase responsiveness.
Large animals or highly active behavior:
Reduce sensitivity to avoid overcompensation and motor jitter.

documents.title: Commutator PRO (Part 38)
documents.text:
Reduce sensitivity to avoid overcompensation and motor jitter.
② Speed knob
Function:
Controls the rotation amplitude and velocity (7 increments) of the compensating motor in response to detected movement.
Effect:
Higher speed settings result in faster and larger motor responses, while lower settings make the compensation gentler and more gradual.
Usage scenario:
For
rapid or vigorous animal movements
, increase speed to prevent cable tension buildup.
For
delicate behaviors

documents.title: Commutator PRO (Part 39)
documents.text:
delicate behaviors
(e.g., head-fixed setups or fine-motor experiments), reduce speed for smoother operation.

Firmware Interface and IMU Reference
The Commutator provides real-time device telemetry and configuration control through a USB UART interface. Starting from firmware version 1.2, the system outputs IMU sensor data, motor position information, and system status using a JSON-based communication protocol.，

IMU Coordinate System

documents.title: Commutator PRO (Part 54)
documents.text:
IMU Coordinate System
The onboard IMU follows the coordinate system defined by the Bosch BNO055 orientation sensor. All orientation, acceleration, and gravity values reported in the telemetry output correspond to this reference frame.
In this coordinate system:
The X-axis represents rotation along the device width direction.
The Y-axis represents forward and backward tilt relative to the device.
The Z-axis represents vertical rotation perpendicular to the device surface.

documents.title: Commutator PRO (Part 55)
documents.text:
The Z-axis represents vertical rotation perpendicular to the device surface.
These axes define the reference frame used for all IMU measurements.
Relationship Between IMU Data and Telemetry Parameters
The telemetry parameters correspond directly to the IMU axes described above.
Orientation
ox — rotation angle around the X-axis
oy — rotation angle around the Y-axis
oz — rotation angle around the Z-axis
Acceleration
gx, gy, gz — gravity vector components along X, Y, Z axes

documents.title: Commutator PRO (Part 56)
documents.text:
gx, gy, gz — gravity vector components along X, Y, Z axes
These values describe the spatial orientation and movement of the commutator during operation.
Motor Response Based on IMU Tilt
The Commutator uses the gravity component along the Y-axis (gy) to determine whether the motor should respond.
During operation, the firmware continuously monitors the device tilt along the Y-axis. If the detected tilt exceeds the configured gy_range threshold, motor movement will be temporarily disabled.

documents.title: Commutator PRO (Part 57)
documents.text:
During operation, the firmware continuously monitors the device tilt along the Y-axis. If the detected tilt exceeds the configured gy_range threshold, motor movement will be temporarily disabled.
This mechanism prevents unstable motor operation when the device is excessively tilted and helps maintain reliable cable management during freely moving experiments.
Command Interface
The firmware supports configuration commands sent through the USB UART interface using JSON format.

documents.title: Commutator PRO (Part 58)
documents.text:
The firmware supports configuration commands sent through the USB UART interface using JSON format.
Commands are applied immediately after they are received.
gy_range Configuration
The gy_range parameter defines the allowable tilt range along the Y-axis before motor movement is disabled.
• Default Value
gy_range = 5
• Range
0 – 10
A larger value requires the device to remain within a smaller tilt range before the motor is allowed to move.
• Example command
{"gy_range":6}

documents.title: Commutator PRO (Part 59)
documents.text:
{"gy_range":6}
This parameter is stored in non-volatile memory and remains unchanged after power cycling.
Data Rate Configuration
The data rate parameter (dr) controls the telemetry output interval.
• Default Value
20 ms
• Range
20 – 5000 ms
• Example command
{"dr":500}
This command sets the telemetry output interval to 500 ms.
The data rate setting is not saved after power cycling and will return to 20 ms after reboot.
NOTE :
• Commands must be sent as valid JSON strings.

documents.title: Commutator PRO (Part 60)
documents.text:
• Commands must be sent as valid JSON strings.
• Only one parameter should be modified per command.
• The gy_range parameter is persistent, while the data rate resets after power cycling.
• Telemetry output continues during configuration commands.

System Verification and IMU Status Monitoring
This section outlines the procedures for establishing a connection between the Commutator Pro and your workstation using CoolTerm, and how to verify the operational integrity of the integrated IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit).
1. Software Installation and Acquisition
To facilitate communication with the Commutator Pro, users are required to utilize the CoolTerm serial terminal utility as the primary interface for system configuration and monitoring.

documents.title: Commutator PRO (Part 62)
documents.text:
To facilitate communication with the Commutator Pro, users are required to utilize the CoolTerm serial terminal utility as the primary interface for system configuration and monitoring.
Official Download Link: Users can access the software at
https://freeware.the-meiers.org/
.
2. Connection Procedures
Follow these steps to link the hardware to your computer:
Physical Setup:
Connect the Commutator Pro to your PC via USB and power on the device.
CoolTerm Configuration:
1. Open CoolTerm.

documents.title: Commutator PRO (Part 63)
documents.text:
1. Open CoolTerm.
2. Click on the Connection menu at the bottom left (as shown in the reference image) or the Options icon in the top toolbar.
3. Ensure the settings are: Baudrate: 115200, Data Bits: 8, Parity: None, and Stop Bits: 1.
4. Click Connect.
3. Interpreting the Startup Sequence
The system performs a self-test upon connection. Use the following logs to verify the status of the IMU sensor.
A. Normal Operational Status (Success)

documents.title: Commutator PRO (Part 64)
documents.text:
A. Normal Operational Status (Success)
A successful connection is confirmed when the terminal displays the initialization sequence below:
1. Sensor Detection:
The string BNO055 detected appears.
2. System Setup:
The log will show IMU Mode 1, Low Power Mode, and the motor initialization (Setup Motor, Init Motor, Motor On).
3.Data Streaming:
Numerical values will begin to scroll, indicating real-time orientation tracking is active.
NOTE:

documents.title: Commutator PRO (Part 65)
documents.text:
NOTE:
It is normal to see several .Failed to send! messages at the very top of the log during the initial handshake. These can be ignored once the BNO055 detected message appears.
B. IMU Disconnected Status (Error)
If the IMU sensor is disconnected or failing to communicate, the terminal will display a repetitive error log:
Error Message:
The screen will fill with Not detected and No BNO055 detected.
System Stall:

documents.title: Commutator PRO (Part 66)
documents.text:
System Stall:
You will see .Failed to send! repeated, and the motor initialization sequence will not start.
4. Troubleshooting the IMU Link
If a "Disconnected" state is observed, please perform the following diagnostic steps before proceeding with your experiment:
1. Physical Inspection:
Ensure the internal sensor or external interface cables are securely connected and haven't been dislodged during setup.
2. Reset Connection:

documents.title: Commutator PRO (Part 67)
documents.text:
2. Reset Connection:
Click Disconnect in CoolTerm and then Connect again to refresh the communication buffer.

documents.title: Standard Commutator HDMI (Part 1)
documents.text:
Standard Commutator HDMI
2026-01-30
Overview
The Standard Commutator HDMI is a passive, low-torque commutator designed for KonteX X-Headstage™ systems. It enables continuous mechanical rotation while maintaining stable electrical connections, minimizing cable twisting and mechanical stress during freely moving animal experiments. The commutator is compatible with XR headstages (up to 256 channels) and XSR headstages (up to 32 channels) using an HDMI-based interface.

documents.title: Standard Commutator HDMI (Part 2)
documents.text:
The Standard Commutator HDMI is a passive, low-torque commutator designed for KonteX X-Headstage™ systems. It enables continuous mechanical rotation while maintaining stable electrical connections, minimizing cable twisting and mechanical stress during freely moving animal experiments. The commutator is compatible with XR headstages (up to 256 channels) and XSR headstages (up to 32 channels) using an HDMI-based interface.
This commutator is intended for use in freely moving or rotating experimental setups, where continuous rotation is required without compromising signal continuity or introducing additional electrical noise.

documents.title: Standard Commutator HDMI (Part 3)
documents.text:
This commutator is intended for use in freely moving or rotating experimental setups, where continuous rotation is required without compromising signal continuity or introducing additional electrical noise.
Commutator STD Overview
The commutator operates as a fully passive device, containing no active electronics or signal conditioning components.
Signal transmission is maintained through internal rotating contacts, allowing unrestricted rotational motion while preserving channel integrity.

documents.title: Standard Commutator HDMI (Part 4)
documents.text:
Signal transmission is maintained through internal rotating contacts, allowing unrestricted rotational motion while preserving channel integrity.
The passive design ensures zero power consumption and eliminates potential noise sources associated with active circuitry.

unit: mm
Key Features
Passive Signal Routing
Fully passive design with no active electronics, ensuring transparent signal transmission without added noise or power requirements.
Low-Torque Continuous Rotation

documents.title: Standard Commutator HDMI (Part 5)
documents.text:
Low-Torque Continuous Rotation
Smooth, low-resistance rotation minimizes cable twisting and mechanical stress during freely moving behavioral experiments.
X-Headstage Compatibility
Compatible with KonteX XR headstages (up to 256 channels) and XSR headstages (up to 32 channels) via an HDMI-based interface.
HDMI High-Density Interface
Standardized HDMI connectors provide reliable, high-density signal routing and ease of system integration.
Optimized Mechanical Design

documents.title: Standard Commutator HDMI (Part 6)
documents.text:
Optimized Mechanical Design
Designed for overhead mounting with durable construction to support repeated rotation and long-term experimental use.
Reduced Cable and Connector Wear
Proper cable routing around the rotating shaft reduces strain, extending cable and connector lifespan.
Assembly Process
① Mounting the Commutator

documents.title: Standard Commutator HDMI (Part 7)
documents.text:
① Mounting the Commutator
Securely mount the Standard Commutator HDMI (Commutator STD) above the behavior box or experimental arena. Ensure the commutator is firmly fixed and aligned vertically to allow smooth, unrestricted rotation during operation.
② Headstage-to-Commutator Connection
Using a compatible HDMI cable, connect the X-Headstage to the Commutator STD. Verify that the connectors are fully seated and properly aligned before proceeding.
③ Cable Routing and Strain Management

documents.title: Standard Commutator HDMI (Part 8)
documents.text:
③ Cable Routing and Strain Management
Route the cable according to the illustrated configuration, wrapping and securing it around the rotating shaft of the Commutator STD as indicated. Leave sufficient cable length to accommodate the animal’s full range of motion while minimizing excess slack that could introduce mechanical drag or tangling.
④ Final Headstage Connection

documents.title: Standard Commutator HDMI (Part 9)
documents.text:
④ Final Headstage Connection
Connect the opposite end of the HDMI cable to the X-Headstage. Confirm that the connection is secure and that the cable routing does not impose axial or lateral forces on the headstage connector.
⑤ Commutator-to-XDAQ Connection
Connect the fixed (non-rotating) end of the Commutator STD to the XDAQ acquisition system using a compatible HDMI cable. Ensure the connection is secure and that the cable is properly strain-relieved.
NOTE:

documents.title: Standard Commutator HDMI (Part 10)
documents.text:
NOTE:
1. Mount the commutator in a vertical orientation to ensure optimal rotational performance.
2. Avoid excessive axial or lateral forces on the HDMI connectors.
3. Ensure sufficient cable slack to allow free rotation without tension.

documents.title: Commutator PRO Firmware Update (Part 1)
documents.text:
Commutator PRO Firmware Update
2026-05-08
Overview
This manual provides the step-by-step procedure to update the firmware on your Commutator Pro. Please ensure you have the correct
.uf2
update file provided by KonteX before proceeding.
Preparation
Ensure your Commutator Pro is powered off before starting the process.
Prepare a high-quality USB cable to connect the device to your PC.
A paperclip or a similar thin tool is required to access the firmware update button.

documents.title: Commutator PRO Firmware Update (Part 2)
documents.text:
A paperclip or a similar thin tool is required to access the firmware update button.
Locate the Firmware Update Button (pinhole) on the device interface.
Step-by-Step Update Procedure
1. Connect to PC: Plug the USB cable from the Commutator Pro's USB port into your computer.
2. Enter Bootloader Mode:
Use a thin tool (like a paperclip) to press and hold the pinhole button.
While holding the button, switch the Power on.

documents.title: Commutator PRO Firmware Update (Part 3)
documents.text:
While holding the button, switch the Power on.
3. Identify Device: Your PC will recognize the device as a new removable drive named "RPI-RP2". You can now release the pinhole button.
4. Deploy Firmware: Drag and drop (or copy) the provided .uf2 firmware file directly into the "RPI-RP2" drive.
5. Installation: Wait for the file transfer to complete. The drive will automatically disappear from your file explorer once the update is processed.

documents.title: Commutator PRO Firmware Update (Part 4)
documents.text:
5. Installation: Wait for the file transfer to complete. The drive will automatically disappear from your file explorer once the update is processed.
6. Finalize Update: Toggle the power switch to Restart the Commutator. Your device is now updated and ready for use.
NOTE:
Do not disconnect the USB cable or power off the device while the drive is active and the file is being transferred, as this may result in firmware corruption.

documents.title: Adpt-MEA2 (Part 1)
documents.text:
Adpt-MEA2
2026-04-23
Overview
MEA (Microelectrode Array) is a simplified electrophysiological signal recording system designed to streamline the electrophysiological recording process. Its design allows users to conveniently swap different types of microelectrode array chips based on specific experimental requirements.
Record electrical signals from multiple sites within the culture.
Replicate the biophysical microenvironment found in vivo.
MEA2 Overview
MEA2 exploded view
Dimension
unit: mm

documents.title: Adpt-MEA2 (Part 2)
documents.text:
unit: mm
Device Overview

documents.title: Adpt-MEA2 (Part 3)
documents.text:
Device Overview
In vitro experiments often struggle to replicate the cellular conditions found in living organisms. Primary cells cultured on rigid multi-electrode arrays (MEAs) exhibit significant differences from their in vivo counterparts, limiting the predictive value of such data. MEAs utilize glass multi-electrode arrays to recreate the mechanical and electrical environments of in vivo cells in a controlled setting. They can be paired with various headstages and stimulation array boards for optical imaging and electrical stimulation/recording, effectively bridging the gap between in vitro and in vivo research and enhancing the relevance of experimental findings.

documents.title: Adpt-MEA2 (Part 4)
documents.text:
In vitro experiments often struggle to replicate the cellular conditions found in living organisms. Primary cells cultured on rigid multi-electrode arrays (MEAs) exhibit significant differences from their in vivo counterparts, limiting the predictive value of such data. MEAs utilize glass multi-electrode arrays to recreate the mechanical and electrical environments of in vivo cells in a controlled setting. They can be paired with various headstages and stimulation array boards for optical imaging and electrical stimulation/recording, effectively bridging the gap between in vitro and in vivo research and enhancing the relevance of experimental findings.
Assembly Process

documents.title: Adpt-MEA2 (Part 5)
documents.text:
Assembly Process
① Remove the ITO conductive glass
② Remove the silicone gasket

④ Separate the top and bottom housings
⑤ Remove and replace the MEA cup
Key Features
Flexibility:
Compatible with various brands and types of microelectrode array chips, enabling users to choose according to their experimental needs.
Efficiency:

documents.title: Adpt-MEA2 (Part 6)
documents.text:
Efficiency:
The simplified system design enhances the efficiency of electrophysiological signal recording, reducing preparation time for experiments.
Data Precision:
MEA captures neuronal activity and electrophysiological signals between cells with high accuracy, facilitating further analysis and research.
User-Friendly:
The interface is designed to be intuitive, suitable for researchers of varying experience levels.
Scalability:

documents.title: Adpt-MEA2 (Part 7)
documents.text:
Scalability:
The system can be expanded as needed, allowing for the addition of more channels to record additional signals.
Experimental Applications
Neuroscience Research:
Used to study interactions between neurons.
Biomedical Research:
Employed in drug testing and research on neurological disorders.
Immunology:
Applicable for investigating cellular signaling and interactions.
The flexibility and efficiency of MEA make it an essential tool in many biomedical research applications.

2025-12-29
Overview
The Adapter X6 to Dual X3 is designed to convert the 128-channel input interface of the X6 headstage into two independent 64-channel X3 input ports. This enables full compatibility with existing X3-series accessories, probes, EIBs, and adapter modules.
The adapter preserves signal integrity through matched routing, ensuring that recording performance remains consistent with native X3 headstage configurations.

- Shielding (S) is connected by default to ground (G). To use an external shielding signal (ES) via the adapter, cut JP4 and solder a wire to the ES pad.

documents.title: XDAQ (Part 1)
documents.text:
XDAQ
XDAQ delivers seamless, native integration with Neuropixels 1.0 and 2.0, supporting high-density recordings of up to 4 probes and 3,072 channels per system. Its high-speed Thunderbolt interface ensures powerful, low-latency performance for real-time analysis and closed-loop neuroscience applications.

documents.title: XDAQ (Part 2)
documents.text:
XDAQ delivers seamless, native integration with Neuropixels 1.0 and 2.0, supporting high-density recordings of up to 4 probes and 3,072 channels per system. Its high-speed Thunderbolt interface ensures powerful, low-latency performance for real-time analysis and closed-loop neuroscience applications.
Fully compatible with standard imec headstages and cables, XDAQ eliminates the need for QBSC cards and PXI enclosures—simplifying setup and reducing system complexity. The XDAQ AIO offers all the functionality of OneBox, while delivering expanded performance and enhanced flexibility for advanced research needs.

documents.title: XDAQ (Part 3)
documents.text:
Fully compatible with standard imec headstages and cables, XDAQ eliminates the need for QBSC cards and PXI enclosures—simplifying setup and reducing system complexity. The XDAQ AIO offers all the functionality of OneBox, while delivering expanded performance and enhanced flexibility for advanced research needs.
As the top-tier model in the XDAQ series, the AIO sets a new standard for versatility and capability in neuroscience instrumentation, offering robust native support for large-scale neural recording, optogenetic stimulation, and synchronized video acquisition. It supports up to 1024 channels of high-resolution spike recordings and, in XSR mode, provides up to 128 channels that can be dynamically configured for recording or stimulation. It also delivers seamless compatibility with advanced electrode technologies—including Neuropixels probes—with the ability to operate up to four probes simultaneously for high-density, multi-region neural interrogation.

documents.text:
XDAQ AIO
2025-12-11
Overview
The XDAQ™ product line offers a suite of acquisition solutions designed to address the evolving needs of modern neuroscience research. These systems combine flexibility and performance, enabling integrated, multimodal approaches to a wide range of experimental techniques.

documents.title: XDAQ AIO (Part 2)
documents.text:
The XDAQ™ product line offers a suite of acquisition solutions designed to address the evolving needs of modern neuroscience research. These systems combine flexibility and performance, enabling integrated, multimodal approaches to a wide range of experimental techniques.
As the top-tier model in the XDAQ series, the AIO sets a new standard for versatility and capability in neuroscience instrumentation, offering robust native support for large-scale neural recording, optogenetic stimulation, and synchronized video acquisition. It supports up to 1024 channels of high-resolution spike recordings and, in XSR mode, provides up to 128 channels that can be dynamically configured for recording or stimulation. It also delivers seamless compatibility with advanced electrode technologies—including Neuropixels probes—with the ability to operate up to four probes simultaneously for high-density, multi-region neural interrogation.

documents.title: XDAQ AIO (Part 3)
documents.text:
As the top-tier model in the XDAQ series, the AIO sets a new standard for versatility and capability in neuroscience instrumentation, offering robust native support for large-scale neural recording, optogenetic stimulation, and synchronized video acquisition. It supports up to 1024 channels of high-resolution spike recordings and, in XSR mode, provides up to 128 channels that can be dynamically configured for recording or stimulation. It also delivers seamless compatibility with advanced electrode technologies—including Neuropixels probes—with the ability to operate up to four probes simultaneously for high-density, multi-region neural interrogation.
With its high-bandwidth architecture, extensive I/O options, and precision timing synchronization, the AIO is purpose-built for real-time, closed-loop experiments, multimodal fusion, and large-scale parallel data acquisition across complex behavioral paradigms.

Setting Up The IO Ports
① Analog BNC Ports
AIO features two pairs of wide range (±10V), general purpose 16-bit ADC (AI-01, AI-02) and DAC (AO-01, AO-02) channels, each equipped with a BNC connector for easy access. These ports operate on the same sample rate as the X-Headstage™ (XR, XSR Mode). In NP mode, the sample rate is fixed at 30kHz.
② Digital BNC Ports

documents.title: XDAQ AIO (Part 7)
documents.text:
② Digital BNC Ports
AIO features two pairs of digital input (DI-01, DI-02) and output (DO-01, DO-02) channels, each equipped with a BNC connector. The digital inputs support both 3.3V and 5V digital signals. The signal level for the BNC digital output ports can be configured via software to either 3.3V or 5V logic high. The digital I/Os operate at the same sample rate as the X-Headstage™ (XR, XSR mode). In NP mode, the sample rate is fixed at 30 kHz.
⚠
IMPORTANT:

documents.title: XDAQ AIO (Part 8)
documents.text:
IMPORTANT:
Do not connect ports to unintended signal type. Permanet damage may occour.
③ Thor Vision USB Port
XDAQ AIO features a on-board hardware video processor capable of capturing up to four UVC camera streams simultaneously, or a single high-speed camera stream at up to 300 FPS. Each video frame is precisely time-stamped with the corresponding neural data acquisition timestamp.

documents.title: XDAQ AIO (Part 9)
documents.text:
XDAQ AIO features a on-board hardware video processor capable of capturing up to four UVC camera streams simultaneously, or a single high-speed camera stream at up to 300 FPS. Each video frame is precisely time-stamped with the corresponding neural data acquisition timestamp.
By utilizing a dedicated hardware video encoding engine, the system ensures reliable, lossless frame capture—eliminating dropped or skipped frames commonly associated with software-based solutions.
④ LED Driver Ports

documents.title: XDAQ AIO (Part 10)
documents.text:
④ LED Driver Ports
XDAQ AIO features 4ch of onboard high-power light source drivers. Drive LED light sources from commercial vendors like Thorlab, Plexon, TDT etc.
Stimulus intensity and modulation can be controlled independently of the main neural acquisition.Each light source driver supports up to 1A of power output and modulation frequency from DC to 10kHz.
⑤ DB25 IO Port

documents.title: XDAQ AIO (Part 11)
documents.text:
⑤ DB25 IO Port
The DB25 connector provides six additional pairs of digital I/O channels. The digital inputs on the DB25 connector (DI-03 to DI-08) are compatible with both 3.3V and 5V digital signals. The digital outputs on the DB25 connector (DO-03 to DO-08) are designed for 3.3V logic high. The digital I/Os operate at the same sample rate as the X-Headstage™ (XR, XSR mode). The connector also includes supplementary device signals for interfacing with external devices.
⑥ Ground Ports

documents.text:

the digital ground shared by the internal electronic circuitry.
Chassis GND —
connected to the metal enclosure, the outer shield of the HDMI cable, and earth ground via the power supply.

documents.title: XDAQ AIO (Part 13)
documents.text:
connected to the metal enclosure, the outer shield of the HDMI cable, and earth ground via the power supply.
As a starting point, we recommend bonding the two ground ports together and using this common node to ground the Faraday cage shielding. Since environmental conditions can vary significantly, users should test and adjust the grounding configuration to determine the most effective setup for their specific environment.
⑦ Expansion Ports

documents.title: XDAQ AIO (Part 14)
documents.text:
⑦ Expansion Ports
The EXP1 and EXP2 ports connect to XDAQ™ accessories, such as the I/O Expander, to provide additional digital and analog I/O functionality.
⑧ 3.5mm Audio Port
This 3.5mm stereo jack enables audio monitoring of two selected headstage amplifier channels, configured via software: Analog Out 1 (AO-01), which is routed to the left audio channel, and Analog Out 2 (AO-02), which is routed to the right audio channel.

Setting Up The Headstage And Probe
For Record-Only (XR) or Stim-Record (XSR) Experiments
1. Connect the Type-D micro-HDMI connector of the stock KonteX HDMI cable to the X-Headstage™.
2. Connect the Type-A HDMI connector to one of the XDAQ™ Headstage Ports. Always use Port 1, as other ports may be unavailable depending on the license purchased with the XDAQ™.

documents.title: XDAQ AIO (Part 16)
documents.text:
2. Connect the Type-A HDMI connector to one of the XDAQ™ Headstage Ports. Always use Port 1, as other ports may be unavailable depending on the license purchased with the XDAQ™.
3. Optionally, a commutator can be used between the headstage and the XDAQ™ Headstage Ports.
4. Plug the electrode into the headstage.
For Neuropixels Experiments (NP Mode)
1. Connect the Omnetics connector of the Neuropixels cable to the headstage.

documents.title: XDAQ AIO (Part 17)
documents.text:
1. Connect the Omnetics connector of the Neuropixels cable to the headstage.
2. Connect the USB-C connector of the cable to one of the Neuropixels Ports (Port 1–4).
3. Optionally, a commutator can be used between the Neuropixels headstage and the XDAQ™. Refer to the Commutator User Manual for more information.

documents.title: XDAQ AIO (Part 18)
documents.text:
3. Optionally, a commutator can be used between the Neuropixels headstage and the XDAQ™. Refer to the Commutator User Manual for more information.
4. Insert the Neuropixels probe into the headstage by connecting the flex cable of the Neuropixels probe to the headstage. Note: The metal pads on the flex cable should face downward when inserting it into the headstage. Flip the flex cable connector down to lock the probe in place.
Installing Driver & Software
1. Connecting to a PC

documents.title: XDAQ AIO (Part 19)
documents.text:
1. Connecting to a PC
Use the supplied Thunderbolt cable to connect the Computer Port on the XDAQ™ to a Thunderbolt port on the PC. The cable is identifiable by the lightning bolt symbol on its connector.
The XDAQ™ can also supply power to the connected computer: CORE2 can deliver up to 15 W, sufficient to power most laptops. The Peripheral Port supplies up to 15 W of power.

documents.title: XDAQ AIO (Part 20)
documents.text:
The XDAQ™ can also supply power to the connected computer: CORE2 can deliver up to 15 W, sufficient to power most laptops. The Peripheral Port supplies up to 15 W of power.
The Peripheral Port can also be used to connect a Thunderbolt accessory, such as a storage device, which will be accessible from the host PC.

Check your firmware version before launching the application

Please refer to the Firmware Update section in this manual for detailed instructions before proceeding with any recording or stimulation experiments.

Please refer to the Firmware Update section in this manual for detailed instructions before proceeding with any recording or stimulation experiments.

documents.title: XDAQ CORE2 (Part 1)
documents.text:
XDAQ CORE2
2025-12-11
Overview
The XDAQ™ product line offers a suite of acquisition solutions designed to address the evolving needs of modern neuroscience research. These systems combine flexibility and performance, enabling integrated, multimodal approaches to a wide range of experimental techniques.

documents.title: XDAQ CORE2 (Part 2)
documents.text:
The XDAQ™ product line offers a suite of acquisition solutions designed to address the evolving needs of modern neuroscience research. These systems combine flexibility and performance, enabling integrated, multimodal approaches to a wide range of experimental techniques.
The CORE2 model is a uniquely versatile platform that unifies multiple capabilities into a single device. It supports up to 512 channels of high-resolution spike recordings and, in XSR mode, provides up to 32 channels that can be dynamically configured for recording or stimulation. Additionally, the system includes native support for advanced electrode technologies, such as Neuropixels probes, with the capacity to operate up to two probes simultaneously.

documents.title: XDAQ CORE2 (Part 3)
documents.text:
The CORE2 model is a uniquely versatile platform that unifies multiple capabilities into a single device. It supports up to 512 channels of high-resolution spike recordings and, in XSR mode, provides up to 32 channels that can be dynamically configured for recording or stimulation. Additionally, the system includes native support for advanced electrode technologies, such as Neuropixels probes, with the capacity to operate up to two probes simultaneously.
With comprehensive onboard and expandable input/output (I/O) capabilities, the XDAQ™ seamlessly integrates with standard laboratory equipments, ensuring compatibility across diverse experimental setups.

Setting Up The IO Ports
① Analog BNC Ports

documents.title: XDAQ CORE2 (Part 8)
documents.text:
① Analog BNC Ports
CORE2 features a set of wide range (±10V), general purpose 16-bit ADC (AI-01) and DAC (AO-01) channels, each equipped with a BNC connector for easy access. These ports operate on the same sample rate as the X-Headstage™ (XR, XSR Mode). In NP mode, the sample rate is fixed at 30kHz.
② Digital BNC Ports

documents.title: XDAQ CORE2 (Part 9)
documents.text:
② Digital BNC Ports
CORE2 features a set of digital input (DI-01) and output (DO-01) channels, each equipped with a BNC connector. The digital inputs support both 3.3V and 5V digital signals. The signal level for the BNC digital output ports can be configured via software to either 3.3V or 5V logic high. The digital I/Os operate at the same sample rate as the X-Headstage™ (XR, XSR mode). In NP mode, the sample rate is fixed at 30 kHz.
⚠
IMPORTANT:

documents.title: XDAQ CORE2 (Part 10)
documents.text:
IMPORTANT:
Do not connect ports to unintended signal type. Permanet damage may occour.
③ DB25 IO Port

documents.title: XDAQ CORE2 (Part 11)
documents.text:
③ DB25 IO Port
The DB25 connector provides six additional pairs of digital I/O channels. The digital inputs on the DB25 connector (DI-03 to DI-08) are compatible with both 3.3V and 5V digital signals. The digital outputs on the DB25 connector (DO-03 to DO-08) are designed for 3.3V logic high. The digital I/Os operate at the same sample rate as the X-Headstage™ (XR, XSR mode). The connector also includes supplementary device signals for interfacing with external devices.
④ Ground Ports
Sys GND —

documents.title: XDAQ CORE2 (Part 12)
documents.text:

the digital ground shared by the internal electronic circuitry.
Chassis GND —
connected to the metal enclosure, the outer shield of the HDMI cable, and earth ground via the power supply.

documents.title: XDAQ CORE2 (Part 13)
documents.text:
connected to the metal enclosure, the outer shield of the HDMI cable, and earth ground via the power supply.
As a starting point, we recommend bonding the two ground ports together and using this common node to ground the Faraday cage shielding. Since environmental conditions can vary significantly, users should test and adjust the grounding configuration to determine the most effective setup for their specific environment.
⑤ Expansion Ports

documents.title: XDAQ CORE2 (Part 14)
documents.text:
⑤ Expansion Ports
The EXP1 and EXP2 ports connect to XDAQ™ accessories, such as the I/O Expander, to provide additional digital and analog I/O functionality.
⑥ 3.5mm Audio Port
This 3.5mm stereo jack enables audio monitoring of two selected headstage amplifier channels, configured via software: Analog Out 1 (AO-01), which is routed to the left audio channel, and Analog Out 2 (AO-02), which is routed to the right audio channel.

4. IO Expander is required to increase the IO support.
5. Latency may vary with OS.

documents.title: XDAQ ONE+ (Part 1)
documents.text:
XDAQ ONE+
2025-12-11
Overview
The XDAQ™ product line offers a suite of acquisition solutions designed to address the evolving needs of modern neuroscience research. These systems combine flexibility and performance, enabling integrated, multimodal approaches to a wide range of experimental techniques.

documents.title: XDAQ ONE+ (Part 2)
documents.text:
The XDAQ™ product line offers a suite of acquisition solutions designed to address the evolving needs of modern neuroscience research. These systems combine flexibility and performance, enabling integrated, multimodal approaches to a wide range of experimental techniques.
The ONE+ model is a uniquely versatile platform that unifies multiple capabilities into a single device. It supports up to 1024 channels of high-resolution spike recordings and, in XSR mode, provides up to 128 channels that can be dynamically configured for recording or stimulation. Additionally, the system includes native support for advanced electrode technologies, such as Neuropixels probes, with the capacity to operate up to four probes simultaneously.

documents.title: XDAQ ONE+ (Part 3)
documents.text:
The ONE+ model is a uniquely versatile platform that unifies multiple capabilities into a single device. It supports up to 1024 channels of high-resolution spike recordings and, in XSR mode, provides up to 128 channels that can be dynamically configured for recording or stimulation. Additionally, the system includes native support for advanced electrode technologies, such as Neuropixels probes, with the capacity to operate up to four probes simultaneously.
With comprehensive onboard and expandable input/output (I/O) capabilities, the XDAQ™ seamlessly integrates with standard laboratory equipments, ensuring compatibility across diverse experimental setups.

① Analog BNC Ports

documents.title: XDAQ ONE+ (Part 8)
documents.text:
① Analog BNC Ports
ONE+ features two pairs of wide range (±10V), general purpose 16-bit ADC (AI-01, AI-02) and DAC (AO-01, AO-02) channels, each equipped with a BNC connector for easy access. These ports operate on the same sample rate as the X-Headstage™ (XR, XSR Mode). In NP mode, the sample rate is fixed at 30kHz.
② Digital BNC Ports

documents.title: XDAQ ONE+ (Part 9)
documents.text:
② Digital BNC Ports
ONE+ features two pairs of digital input (DI-01, DI-02) and output (DO-01, DO-02) channels, each equipped with a BNC connector. The digital inputs support both 3.3V and 5V digital signals. The signal level for the BNC digital output ports can be configured via software to either 3.3V or 5V logic high. The digital I/Os operate at the same sample rate as the X-Headstage™ (XR, XSR mode). In NP mode, the sample rate is fixed at 30 kHz.

③ DB25 IO Port

documents.title: XDAQ ONE+ (Part 11)
documents.text:
③ DB25 IO Port
The DB25 connector provides six additional pairs of digital I/O channels. The digital inputs on the DB25 connector (DI-03 to DI-08) are compatible with both 3.3V and 5V digital signals. The digital outputs on the DB25 connector (DO-03 to DO-08) are designed for 3.3V logic high. The digital I/Os operate at the same sample rate as the X-Headstage™ (XR, XSR mode). The connector also includes supplementary device signals for interfacing with external devices.

documents.text:
connected to the metal enclosure, the outer shield of the HDMI cable, and earth ground via the power supply.
As a starting point, we recommend bonding the two ground ports together and using this common node to ground the Faraday cage shielding. Since environmental conditions can vary significantly, users should test and adjust the grounding configuration to determine the most effective setup for their specific environment.
⑤ Expansion Ports

documents.title: XDAQ ONE+ (Part 14)
documents.text:
⑤ Expansion Ports
The EXP1 and EXP2 ports connect to XDAQ™ accessories, such as the I/O Expander, to provide additional digital and analog I/O functionality.
⑥ 3.5mm Audio Port
This 3.5mm stereo jack enables audio monitoring of two selected headstage amplifier channels, configured via software: Analog Out 1 (AO-01), which is routed to the left audio channel, and Analog Out 2 (AO-02), which is routed to the right audio channel.

Connect the Type-A HDMI connector to one of the XDAQ™ Headstage Ports. Always use Port 1, as other ports may be unavailable depending on the license purchased with the XDAQ™.

Optionally, a commutator can be used between the headstage and the XDAQ™ Headstage Ports.
Plug the electrode into the headstage.

Insert the Neuropixels probe into the headstage by connecting the flex cable of the Neuropixels probe to the headstage. Note: The metal pads on the flex cable should face downward when inserting it into the headstage. Flip the flex cable connector down to lock the probe in place.

2026-06-26
Overview
The X3 Headstage Stimulation Test Module is designed to verify the stimulus waveform generated by X3SR headstages. By connecting the module to a dummy 1 kΩ resistive load, users can safely observe and validate stimulation output characteristics without connecting to biological tissue.

Overview
The X-Headstage™ Removal Tool is a custom-designed flat-head tool with tips specifically shaped to interface with both X3 and X6 headstages. It enables safe and controlled removal of headstages from connected accessories or adapters.
Operation
Use the included Phillips screwdriver to remove the screws securing the X-Headstage.
Insert the flat-head tip into the designated removal slot on the headstage interface.
Apply gentle, even force to disengage the connector.

documents.title: XHS-Tool (Part 2)

Apply gentle, even force to disengage the connector.
Avoid twisting or prying at an angle to prevent mechanical stress on the connector or PCB.

• Do not apply excessive force during removal.
• Ensure the system is powered off before removing the headstage.

The Fine-Cable Organizer Kit is designed for use with the Commutator Pro to manage fine cables with lengths of 1 m, 2.5 m, or 5 m.

documents.title: Cable-IMU (Part 2)
documents.text:
The Commutator IMU Cable is a dedicated communication cable designed to interface an external Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) sensor with the KonteX Commutator system. The cable serves as the communication link that enables real-time transmission of motion and orientation data from the IMU sensor to the Commutator controller.
During freely moving experiments, animals may continuously rotate, causing tethered cables to twist and accumulate torque. The IMU sensor monitors the animal’s rotational movement using gyroscopic measurements and transmits this information through the IMU Cable to the Commutator. Based on the detected direction and magnitude of rotation, the Commutator automatically rotates in the corresponding direction, preventing cable twisting and maintaining a low-torque environment throughout the experiment.

documents.title: Cable-IMU (Part 3)
documents.text:
During freely moving experiments, animals may continuously rotate, causing tethered cables to twist and accumulate torque. The IMU sensor monitors the animal’s rotational movement using gyroscopic measurements and transmits this information through the IMU Cable to the Commutator. Based on the detected direction and magnitude of rotation, the Commutator automatically rotates in the corresponding direction, preventing cable twisting and maintaining a low-torque environment throughout the experiment.
By actively compensating for rotational motion, the Commutator IMU system helps preserve natural animal behavior, reduces mechanical stress on implants and cables, and improves the reliability of long-duration recording sessions.

documents.title: Cable-IMU (Part 4)
documents.text:
By actively compensating for rotational motion, the Commutator IMU system helps preserve natural animal behavior, reduces mechanical stress on implants and cables, and improves the reliability of long-duration recording sessions.
The IMU Cable is dedicated to motion-sensing communication and Commutator control functions. It does not carry neural recording signals, stimulation signals, video data, or other experimental data streams.

documents.title: Tester-Z-X3 (Part 1)
documents.text:
Tester-Z-X3
2026-06-26
Overview
The X3 Headstage 64-Channel Impedance Test Module is designed to evaluate the impedance measurement functionality of X3 headstages. By connecting the module to dummy test loads with resistive and capacitive (R/C) values ranging from kΩ to MΩ, users can verify impedance measurement accuracy without connecting to electrodes or biological tissue.
Tester-Z-X3 Overview
Test Operation

documents.title: Tester-Z-X3 (Part 2)
documents.text:
Test Operation
Due to the keyed orientation of the connector pins, impedance testing must be performed in left and right orientations separately. Users should alternate the connection orientation of the X3 Headstage 64-Channel Impedance Test Module to verify impedance measurements on both sides, ensuring complete and accurate validation.

documents.title: Cable-HDMI (Part 1)
documents.text:
Cable-HDMI
2026-03-23
Overview
The HDMI A to D Cable (3M) is a high-quality data transmission cable designed to connect devices with a standard HDMI Type-A port to equipment requiring a HDMI Type-D (Micro HDMI) interface. With a 3-meter length, this cable provides flexible routing for experimental setups where distance between devices is required, such as between the XDAQ system and headstage adapters or commutators.

documents.text:

documents.text:
EIB
The KonteX Electrode Interface Board (EIB) series provides a reliable and modular interface between neural probes and KonteX X-Headstage™ systems.
Designed to ensure stable electrical connectivity, flexible probe integration, and efficient experimental configuration, the EIB platform supports a wide range of electrode formats and recording architectures used in modern neuroscience research.
